Facts.text: Roman senator Petronius Maximus proclaimed Emperor
Crusades complete the massacre of Jews in York, England
Battle of Los Alporchones: forces of Kingdoms of Castile and Murcia defeat Emirate of Granada near Lorca, part of the Spanish Reconquista
French troops invade Flanders
PrinceWilliam I of Orangewelcomed in Amsterdam

First recorded Saint Patrick's Day celebration in America is held in St. Augustine in Spanish Florida, with the first parade held in the same place in 1601. Both are organized by Catholic Irish vicar Ricardo Artur.
William IV Prince of Orange appointed mayor of Drente
Charitable Irish Society of Boston organizes the first observance of Saint Patrick's Day in the Thirteen Colonies
St. Patrick's Day is first celebrated in NYC at the Crown & Thistle Tavern
Prince Mas Saïd of Mataram surrenders to Mangkubumi in Java
First Saint Patrick's Day parade is held in NYC
Transylvania Land Company, headed by Richard Henderson, buys most of Kentucky through treaty signed with Cherokee chiefs at Sycamore Shoals on the Watauga River (later declared illegal)
British forces evacuate Boston to Nova Scotia during Revolutionary War
Britain declares war on France, due to French alliance with US
British warship HMS Queen Charlotte catches fire off the Tuscan Archipelago; 700 die
Friedrich Schiller's play "Wilhelm Tell" premieres
Britain & Netherlands sign a trade agreement
Phoenix Society forms (NY)
Treaty of 1842: Wyandotte (Huron) Indian nation cedes 114,000 acres of land in Ohio and Michigan to US, in exchange for 148,000 acres west of the Mississippi
Bristol man Henry Jones patents self-raising flour
The rubber band is patented by Stephen Perry of London
1st park land purchased by a US city in Worcester, Massachusetts
Battle of Kelly's Ford, Virginia with 211 causalities
Postage stamp canceling machine patent issued
Mass legislature authorizes incorporation of Wellesley Female Seminary
National Association of Professional Base-Ball players organized
British high jump champion Marshall Jones Brooks clears 1.83m at Oxford, England for unofficial world record; thought to be first leap over 6'
General Crook destroy Cheyennes & Oglala-Sioux Indian camps
Australia's English born all-rounder Billy Midwinter completes Test cricket's first 5-wicket haul, 5-78 in the first ever Test match v England in Melbourne
John Joseph Montgomery makes 1st glider flight at Otay, California
Carrollton Massacre (Mississippi) 20 African Americans killed
British Steamer "Utopia" sinks off Gibraltar killing 574
Dominion Hockey Challenge Cup (Stanley Cup): Montreal Hockey Club claim inaugural trophy after finishing top of final Amateur Hockey Association of Canada standings with a 7–1–0 record
US & China sign treaty preventing Chinese laborers from entering US
Windsor luxury hotel in NYC catches fire, 92 die
In South Africa, British troops relieve Mafeking, besieged by the Boers since 13 October, 1899
Free-thinking Democratic League forms in Netherlands
Stanley Cup, Dey's Arena, Ottawa, ON: ECAHA playoff: Montreal Wanderers lose, 9-3 to Ottawa HC but win challenge series, 12-10 on aggregate
The Phi Kappa Tau Fraternity is founded at Miami University in Oxford, Ohio
Camp Fire Girls organization announced by Mrs Luther Halsey Gulick
Uruguayan Air Force is founded
1st exclusively women's bowling tournament begins in St Louis, Missouri
Delta Phi Epsilon is founded at New York University Law School
Dutch steel workers strike for 8 hr day & minimum wages
Dr Marie Stopes opens Britain's 1st birth control clinic in London
Sailors revolt in Kronstadt (thousands die)
The Second Republic of Poland adopts the March Constitution
Diplomats are exchanged between Sweden and the USSR after Sweden recognizes the USSR (March 15)
Netherlands & USSR begin talks over USSR recognition
Dutch Calvinists oust Rev J G Geelkerken over Genesis 3
Spain & Brazil prevent Germany joining League of Nations
US government doesn't sign League of Nations disarmament treaty
Spanish dictator Primo de Rivera closes university of Madrid
Construction begins of the Empire State Building, the world's 1st skyscraper of 100+ stories, on 5th Avenue in New York City
KSO-AM in Des Moines Iowa call sign is given to KWCR
Battle of Nanchang between the Kuomintang and the Japanese breaks out during the Second Sino-Japanese War (1937-1945)
Bełżec Concentration Camp opens with the transport of 30,000 Polish Jews from Lublin
Aldemarin (Ned) & Fort Cedar Lake (US) torpedoed & sinks
F. Hugh Herbert's "Kiss & Tell" premieres in NYC
Physician Willem J. Kolff performs the world's first 'hemodialysis' using his artificial kidney machine, however the treatment is unsuccessful and the patient dies, in the Netherlands
Mount Vesuvius in Italy erupts after months of volcanic unrest, destroying several towns near the volcano
Allied ships bomb North-Sumatra
Element 98 (Californium) announced
New Dutch government of Willem Drees takes power
Test cricket debut of great English fast bowler Brian Statham, England v NZ at Christchurch; goes on to play 70 Tests, 252 wickets @ 24.84
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
WBAY TV channel 2 in Green Bay, WI (CBS) begins broadcasting
WWLP TV channel 22 in Springfield, MA (NBC) begins broadcasting
Dutch ban on Sunday driving lifted
US Navy launches Vanguard 1 into orbit (2nd US), measures Earth shape
Australia & USSR restore diplomatic relations
WSLA (now WAKA) TV channel 8 in Selma, AL (CBS) begins broadcasting
New York City District Attorney arrests professional gamblers who implicate Seton Hall players
South Africa leaves British Commonwealth
Eruptions of Mount Agung on Bali, kills 1,900 Balinese
Beatles announce their second film is titled "8 Arms to Hold You" ; later changed to "Help!"
South Africa's government bans Defense & Aid Fund
US submarine locates missing hydrogen bomb on the Mediterranean sea floor
British harpist Shiela Bromberg plays with string section for the Sgt. Pepper track "She's Leaving Home", becomes the first female to perform on a Beatles recording
2-tiered gold price negotiated in Washington, D.C. by US & 6 European nations
Peter O'Malley becomes CEO of LA Dodgers
US casts their 1st UN Security Council veto (Support England)
Government of Trygve Bratteli takes office in Norway
St. Patrick's Day marchers carry 14 coffins commemorating Bloody Sunday
Valeri Muratov skates world record 1000m (1:16.92)
4 Catholic civilians (including 2 children) are killed and twelve wounded when the Ulster Volunteer Force explode a car bomb at Hillcrest Bar, Dungannon
Boxer Rubin "Hurricane" Carter is retried in the US after being convicted of murder in 1967 (sentence is upheld but overturned 1985)
Malikov skates world record 1000m (1:15.76)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Australia wins cricket Centenary Test by 45 runs, same result as 1877
Amoco Cadiz tanker spills 1.6 mil gallons of oil off French coast
Reds don green uniforms for St Patricks Day
Ice Dance Championship at Vienna won by N Linichuk & G Karponosov USSR
Ice Pairs Championship at Vienna won by Tai Babilonia & R Gardner USA
Men's Figure Skating Champ in Vienna won by Vladimir Kovalev (USSR)
The Penmanshiel Tunnel in Berwickshire, Scotland, collapses during engineering works, killing two workers
Worlds Ladies' Figure Skating Champ in Vienna won by Linda Fratianne
FC Lisse, Dutch soccer team forms
4 Dutch TV crew members shot dead in El Salvador
Haemers gang robs gold transport in Belgium of 35 million BF
IBM releases PC-DOS version 3.3
Iran says Iraq uses poison gas
Then highest scoring NCAA basketball game to date; Loyola-Marymont 119, Wyoming 115
Dorothy Cudahy is 1st female grand marshal of St. Patrick Day Parade
PBA National Championship Won by Jim Pencak
9 of 15 Soviet reps officially approve new union treaty
Irish Lesbians & Gays march in St. Patrick's Day parade
John Robin Baitz' "Substance of Fire" premieres in NYC
New Jersey raises turnpike tolls 70%
Islamic Jihad car bombing of Israeli Embassy in Buenos Aires, Argentina kills 29
Russian manned space craft TM-14, launches into orbit
South African referendum of white voters on an end to apartheid, over two-thirds vote for an end to the system
86 killed by bomb attack in Calcutta
Iran transport aircraft crashes in Azerbaijan (32 killed)
It is announced there is no smoking in Cleveland Indians new ballpark
British pound hits 2.4545 to Dutch guilder (record)
US approves 1st chicken pox vaccine, Varivax by Merck & Co

ICC Men's Cricket ODI World Cup, Gaddafi Stadium, Lahore, Pakistan: Sri Lanka upsets Australia by 7 wickets for their first title; Player of the Match: Aravinda de Silva (SL) 107 no (124), 3/42 & 2 catches
CNN begin Spanish broadcasts
The 800+ deaths of members of the Ugandan cult Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments of God is considered to be a mass murder and suicide orchestrated by leaders of the cult
OPEC decides to cut output by 4% or 1 million barrels per day, effective April 1
British Cabinet Minister Robin Cook, resigns over government plans for the war with Iraq.
Unrest in Kosovo results in more than 22 killed, 200 wounded, and the destruction of 35 Serb Orthodox shrines in Kosovo and two mosques in Belgrade and Nis.
Governor Eliot Spitzer of New York resigns after scandal involving a high-end prostitute; David Paterson becomes acting governor
The popular online game for children, Fantage, is launched
Bolton Wanderer footballer Fabrice Muamba collapses and is rushed to hospital during a live football match against Tottenham Hotspur
MESSENGER spacecraft begins its extended mission
10 people are killed by a car bombing in Basra, Iraq
Toyo Ito wins the 2013 Pritzker Architecture Prize
The Republic of Crimea is declared
Archaeologists announce the discovery of an 2,500 year old iron age warrior king burial ground, with 75 graves in Pocklington, Northern England
Africa's only female head of state, Mauritian President Ameenah Gurib-Fakim confirms she is resigning amid an expenses scandal
China's National People's Congress appoints Wang Qishan as vice-president
Facebook removes 1.5 million videos of the Christchurch mosque shootings in first 24 hrs after the attack, although only 1.2 million blocked at upload
Flash flooding and a landslides kills at least 73 and injures about 60 in Sentani and Jayapura, Papua, eastern Indonesia
US Senator Kirsten Gillibrand announces her presidential bid
Zimbabwe declares a state of emergency after Cyclone Idai tears through eastern Zimbabwe killing at least 259
Chad begins repaying a $100 million debt to Angola with cattle, as more than 1,000 cows arrive in Luanda
European Union announces a 30-day ban on entering its 26 countries for almost all travelers as it struggles to contain COVID-19
Golden Raspberry Film Awards (Razzies): Musical "Cats" wins six awards including worst film
UEFA announces the postponement of its flagship national team football competition, UEFA EURO 2020, due to be played in June / July due to the COVID-19 emergency
Claude Giroux, NHL Philadelphia Flyers longest-serving captain, becomes only 2nd player in franchise history to play 1,000th game for the team, joining Bobby Clarke
"Quiet on Set: The Dark Side of Kids TV" documentary detailing the toxic nature of some Nickelodeon shows, especially under Dan Schneider during the 1990s, early 2000s, premieres
Rio de Janeiro posts record heat index of 62.3 C (144.1 F) - i.e. taking into account humidity, highest in Brazil in a decade (actual temperature 42 C)
WPL Cricket Final, Arun Jaitley Stadium, New Delhi: Royal Challengers Bengaluru beat Delhi Capitals by 8 wickets; Player of the match: Sophie Molineux (RCB) 3/20
In his last victory,Julius Caesardefeats the Pompeian forces of Titus Labienus and Pompey the Younger in the Battle of Munda
Roman EmperorMarcus Aureliusdies leaving his sonCommodusaged 18 as sole emperor
Edward the Black Princeis made Duke of Cornwall, the first Duchy made in England
French KingFrancis Ifreed from Spain
Final performance by famous clownJoseph Grimaldiat a benefit performance at Sadler's Wells, London
Six years after the forcible ending of Japan's isolationist policy by US Navy CommodoreMatthew C. Perry, the Japanese Embassy arrives in San Francisco to sign a Treaty of Friendship
Italy is unified into a single kingdom underVictor Emmanuel IIfollowing the campaigns led byGiuseppe Garibaldi
British boxerBob FitzsimmonsKOs American champion 'Gentleman' Jim Corbett in the 14th round to win the World Heavyweight title in Carson City, Nevada
John Philip Hollandachieves a successful test run for the first modern submarine off Staten Island, submerging for 1 hour and 40 minutes
American modern dancerIsadora Duncangives her first Europen performance in London, England
At a show in Paris, 71 ofVincent van Gogh's paintings cause a sensation 11 years after his death
Albert Einsteinfinishes his scientific paper detailing his quantum theory of light, a foundation of modern physics
Canadian championTommy BurnsKOs Irish challenger Jem Roche in 1:28s of the 1st round at the Theatre Royal, Dublin; then quickest world heavyweight boxing title fight
NY gangsterAlbert Anastasiais convicted of murdering longshoreman George Turino
Vladimir Leninproclaims the New Economic Policy to help the country recover from Russian Civil War
Eugene O'Neill's play "Welded" premieres in NYC
General Motors acquires German auto manufacturerAdam Opel
German police raidAdolf Hitler's Nazi headquarters
Montreal Canadiens centerHowie Morenzregisters his 334th career point with an assist in a 10-4 win v NY Americans; passes Cy Denneny as NHL leader for career points
Austrian Chancellor Engelbert Dollfuss, Italian Prime MinisterBenito Mussolini, and Hungarian Prime Minister Gyula Gömbös sign Donau Pact (Protocols of Rome) guaranteeing Austrian independence
US GeneralDouglas MacArthurarrives in Australia to become supreme commander
English historianArnold J. Toynbeeappears on the front cover of Time magazine
Belgian government ofGaston Eyskensresigns due to constitutional crisis
Bill Veecksays he will sell his 80% of St Louis Browns for $2,475M

AfterMaurice Richardis suspended for the remainder of the season, riots break out in Montreal. 37 people are injured and over 100 are arrested. The following morning, Richard goes on the radio to ask citizens to stop vandalizing the city.
8th Emmy Awards: "TheEd SullivanShow", "ThePhil SilversShow". Phil Silvers &Lucille Ballwin
Presidential plane crashes on Mt. Manunggal in Cebu, Philippines killing 25 including Filipino PresidentRamon Magsaysay
One of the first French New Wave films, "Breathless", directed byJean-Luc Godard, starringJean-Paul Belmondoand Jean Seberg is released in France
US PresidentEisenhowerforms anti-Castro-exile army under the CIA
Elizabeth Ann Setonof NY beatified (canonized in 1975)
Bee Geesmake their US TV debut on "TheEd SullivanShow," performing "To Love Somebody" and "Words"
Golda Meirbecomes Israel's 4th Prime Minister, the first and only female to hold the office
Ringo Starrreleases single "Back off Boogaloo" in the UK
Queen Elizabeth IIopens new London Bridge

Shah of IranMohammad Reza Pahlaviand a consortium of Western firms led by British Petroleum agree to the formal nationalization of Iran's oil industry in return for an assured 20-year supply of Iranian oil
70th hat trick in NHL New York Islander franchise history, scored byMike Bossy
Matti Nykanen of Finland set a world ski jump record of 623'
South African PresidentF. W. de Klerkwins a white only referendum
Sinn Féin leaderGerry Adamsvisits the White House in Washington, D.C.
Pope Francisdelivers his first Angelus prayer and blessing
Stacy Lewiswins the RR Donnelley LPGA Founders Cup and becomes the number one ranked woman golfer in the world
Sia releases her single "Chandelier" with a music video featuringMaddie Ziegler
Brazilian federal judge blocks swearing-in of Luiz InacioLula da Silvaas Chief of Staff to PresidentDilma Rousseff, as part of "Car Wash" controversy
Live-action remake "Beauty and the Beast" directed by Bill Condon and starringEmma Watsonopens
Brazilian PresidentJair Bolsonaroarrives in the US for face-to-face meetings withDonald Trump
America's Cup, Auckland: EmiratesTeam New Zealandbeats Italian yacht Luna Rossa Prada Pirelli by 46s in race 10 for a 7-3 series victory and retain the cup
Nigerian PresidentMuhammadu Buhariapologies for recent fuel shortages and power outages, including the failure of the national electricity grid and an increase in adulterated fuel

After suppressing and murdering opponents, PresidentVladimir Putinclaims a post-Soviet record election victory, with 87.8% of the vote. The six year term will make him Russia's longest-serving leader in over 200 years.
Saint Patrick, aged about 16, is captured by Irish pirates from his home in Great Britain and taken as a slave to Ireland (traditional date)
The Italian Air Force, in support ofFrancisco Franco, bombs Barcelona during the Spanish Civil War
Shijō, 87th Emperor of Japan (1232-42), first son of Emperor Go-Horikawa, born in Japan (d. 1242)
Francesco Albana, Italian painter (Mary's Ascension), born in Bologna (d. 1660)
Daniel van Papenbroeck, Flemish historian, born in Antwerp, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1714)
François Girardon, French sculptor known for his statues and busts of Louis XIV, born in Troyes, Champagne, France (d. 1715)
Georg Österreich, German Baroque composer (founded the Bokemeyer collection), baptized in Magdeburg, Brandenburg-Prussia (d. 1735)
Élisabeth Jacquet de La Guerre, French harpsichordist, organist, singer, and composer (Les pièces de clavessin; Céphale et Procris), born in Paris, France (d. 1729)
Petrus Laurentius Wockenfuss, Polish composer, born in Bruskowo Wielkie, Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth (d. 1721)
Thomas Boston, Scottish church leader, theologian and scholar (Human Nature in Its Fourfold State), born at Duns, Scotland (d. 1732)
Jean-Marc Nattier, French portrait painter (painted Louis XV's court), born in Paris, France (d. 1766)
Antoine Court, French minister and itinerant preacher in the Reformed church who restored Protestantism to France, born in Villeneuve-de-Berg, France (d. 1760)
Lachlan McIntosh, Scottish-American military and political leader during the American Revolution who shot founding father Button Gwinnett in a duel, born in Raits, Badenoch, Scotland (d. 1806)
Nicolas Séjan, French organist and composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1819)
Jan Dawid Holland, Polish composer, born in Sankt Andreasberg, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1827)
Madame Roland [Marie-Jeanne Phlippon], French revolutionary (member of the Girondist faction), born in Paris, France (d. 1793)
Ninian Edwards, American politician (Governor of Illinois Territory, 1809–1813; U.S. Senator from Illinois, 1818–1824; Governor of Illinois 1826–1830), born in Montgomery County, Maryland (d. 1833)
Roger B. Taney, 5th Chief Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court (1836-64) who delivered the majority Dred Scott decision, born in Calvert, Maryland (d. 1864)
Thomas Chalmers, Scottish minister and 1st moderator (Free Church of Scotland 1843-47), born in Anstruther, Scotland (d. 1847)
Dominique Jacques de Eerens, Dutch general and politician (Governor-General of Dutch East Indies 1836-40), born in Alkmaar, Dutch Republic (d. 1840)
Ebenezer Elliott, British poet known as the "Corn Law Rhymer'" for his opposition to the corn laws, born in Masborough, England (d. 1849)
Edmund Kean, British Shakespearean tragic actor (Shylock), born in London, England (d. 1833)
James Bridger, American scout, fur trader and mountain man par excellence, born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 1881)
Norbert Rillieux, American chemical engineer and inventor (sugar refiner), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1894)
Jean Ingelow, English poet and children's novelist (Mopsa the Fairy), born in Boston, Lincolnshire (d. 1897)
Patrick Edward Connor, Irish-American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in County Kerry, Republic of Ireland (d. 1891)
Oskar Peschel, German geographer (classified men into seven races in his book "The Races of Man: and their geographical distribution"), born in Dresden, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1875)
Patrick Ronayne Cleburne, Irish-American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Ovens, Ireland (d. 1864)
Moncure Daniel Conway, American minister, abolitionist and writer (Life of Thomas Paine), born in Stafford County, Virginia (d. 1907)[1]
Walter Quintin Gresham, American statesman, jurist, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Lanesville, Indiana (d. 1895)
Josef Rheinberger, German organist, composer, and educator (Munich Conservatory, 1877-1901), born in Vaduz, Liechtenstein (d. 1901)
Kate Greenaway, English artist known for her children's book illustrations (Under the Window), born in London (d. 1901)
Horace Nicholl, British composer, born in Tipton, England (d. 1922)
Mikhail Vrubel, Russian Symbolist painter, one of the first modern Russian artists (The Demon Cast Down), born in Omsk, Russia (d. 1910)
Silvio Gesell, German-Argentine radical economist who invented a form of money (free money) with an expiration date, born in Sankt Vith, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1930)[1]
Homer Plessy, American Creole shoemaker and civil rights pioneer (plaintiff in landmark U.S. Supreme Court case Plessy v. Ferguson 1896), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1925)
Pierce Butler, American jurist, Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court (1923-39), born in Northfield, Minnesota (d. 1939)
Horace Donisthorpe, British entomologist (British Ants), born in Leicester, England (d. 1951)
Douglas Carr, England cricket spin bowler (1 Test, 7 wickets; Kent CCC), born in Cranbrook, England (d. 1950)
Willie Quaife, English cricket batsman (7 Tests, 1 x 50; Sussex CCC, Warwickshire CCC), born in Newhaven, England (d. 1951)
Margaret Bondfield, British politician and 1st British female cabinet minister (1929-31), born in Chard, Somerset (d. 1953)
Kincsem, Hungarian horse that never lost a race, born in Kisbér, Hungary (d. 1877)
Stephen Samuel Wise, American Rabbi and President of Zionist Org of America, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1949)
Frederic Ayres, American composer (From the Plains), born in Binghamton, New York (d. 1926)
Albert Hahn, Dutch political cartoonist (Het Volk), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1918)
Sid Grauman, American entrepreneur, established the Chinese Theatre and Egyptian Theatre, born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1950)
Guillermo Uribe Holguin, Colombian composer, born in Bogotá, Colombia (d. 1971)
Lawrence Oates, English army officer and Antarctic explorer who sacrificed his life on the Terra Nova Expedition, born in Putney, England (d. 1912)
Patrick Hastings, British politician and barrister regarded as the leading barrister of his time (Case of the Hooded Man and the Elvira Barney murder case), born in London (d. 1952)
Kristian Elster, Norwegian author (Less bror Harris), born in Trondheim, Norway (d. 1947)
Walter Rudolf Hess, Swiss physiologist, Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 1949 for mapping parts of the brain that control internal organs, born in Frauenfeld, Switzerland (d. 1973)[1]
Urmuz, [Demetru Dem. Demetrescu-Buzău], Romanian writer (Bizarre Pages), born in Curtea de Argeș, Romania (d. 1923)
Alcide Nunez, American jazz clarinetist, born in St. Bernard Parish, Louisiana (d. 1934)
Patricia of Connaught, British princess, granddaughter of Queen Victoria, born at Buckingham Palace, London (d. 1974)
Frank Buck, American actor (Africa Screams, Tiger Queen, Tiger Fangs), born in Gainesville, Texas (d. 1950)
Paul Ramadier, French politician and lawyer (Prime Minister 1947), born in La Rochelle (d. 1961)
Harold Morris, American composer, born in San Antonio Texas (d. 1964)
Sayed Darwish, Egyptian singer-songwriter and "father of Egyptian popular music" (Ana Haweet), born in Kôm el-Dikka, Alexandria, Egypt (d. 1923)
Paul Green, American novelist and playwright (In Abraham's Bosom), born in Lillingotn, North Carolina (d. 1981)
Jozef Mazur, American stained glass artist and painter, born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1970)

Alfred Newman, American film music arranger, conductor and composer, winner of 9 Academy Awards (The Robe; How The West Was Won; Love Is A Many-Splendored Thing), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1970)
Piotr Perkowski, Polish composer, born in Oweczacze (now Druzhne), Ukraine (d. 1990)
Patrick Hamilton, English author and playwright (Gaslight, Rope), born in Hassocks, England (d. 1962)
Brigitte Helm [Gisele Eve von Kuenheim], German actress (Gloria, Gold, Metropolis), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1996)
Michael O'Shea, American actor (It's a Great Life, Lady of Burlesque), born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1973)
Tamara Geva, Russian-born dancer and actress, born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1997)
Jean Van Houtte, Belgian politician (Prime Minister of Belgium, 1952-56; Finance Minister, 1950-52 and 1958-61), born in Ghent, Belgium (d. 1991)
Boris Polevoy, Russian journalist and writer, born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1981) [OS=Mar 4]
Radie Britain, American composer, born near Silverton, Texas (d. 1994)
Lovie Lee [Edward Lee Watson], American electric blues pianist, singer, and songwriter, born in Chattanooga, Tennessee (d. 1997)
Patrick Reilly, British diplomat, ambassador to Paris and Moscow who stopped the double agent Kim Philby becoming head of the UK Secret Service, born in Ooty, British India (d. 1999)[1]
Raffaele d'Alessandro, Swiss pianist, organist, and composer, born in St. Gallen, Switzerland (d. 1959)
Charles Lampkin, American character actor (Frank’s Place - "Tiger Shepin"; Roots: The Next Generation), spoken word artist, musician, and educator, born in Montgomery, Alabama (d. 1989)[1]
Bill Roycroft, Australian equestrian (Olympic gold team event 1960, bronze 1968, 76), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2011)
Hans Namuth, German-born photographer (Todos Santos, Guatemala), born in Essen, Germany (d. 1990)
Henry Bumstead, American cinematic art director (2 Oscars: "To Kill a Mockingbird" 1963; "The Sting" 1974), born in Ontario, California (d. 2006)[1][2]
David Stafford-Clark, English psychiatrist, born in Bromley, Kent, England (d. 1999)
Ray Ellington [Henry Pitts Brown], British drummer, bandleader, and singer (The Goon Show), born in Kensington, England (d. 1985)
Brian Boydell, Irish composer, born in Howth, Ireland (d. 2000)
Józef Franczak, Polish soldier and anti-communist resistance fighter who was the last cursed soldiers, born in Kozice Górne, Kingdom of Poland (d. 1963)
Hank Sauer, American baseball left fielder (NL MVP, HR leader, RBI leader 1952; MLB All Star 1950, 52; Chicago Cubs), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2001)
John La Montaine, American composer (Pulitzer 1959), born in Oak Park, Illinois (d. 2013)
Donald Barron, Scottish accountant and CEO (Midland Bank), born in Gorebridge, Midlothian, Scotland (d. 2015)
Mick Harvey, Australian cricketer (in Newcastle Brother of Neil Test umpire), born in Newcastle, Australia (d. 2016)
Patrick Sergeant, British financial journalist and founder of Euromoney Publications, born in Kent, England (d. 2024)
Stephen Dodgson, British composer, born in Chelsea, London, England (d. 2013)
Gabriele Ferzetti, Italian film and stage actor (L'Avventura), born in Rome, Italy (d. 2015)
Arthur Hockaday, British civil servant (Defence Ministry; Director-General  of Commonwealth War Graves Commission. 1982-89), born in Plymouth, England (d. 2004)
Siegfried Lenz, German writer (Das Feuerschiff  - The Lightship; Ein Kriegsende), dramatist, and interpreter for British military (WWII), born in Lyck, East Prussia (d. 2014)
Betty Allen, American opera and contemporary classical mezzo-soprano, educator, and arts administrator, born in Campbell, Ohio (d. 2009)
Nancy Sheehan, Amercian writer (The Heart's Journey; Dead Center), born in the USA
Patrick Allen, British actor (Roman Holiday;Dial "M" for Murder), born in Nyasaland, Africa (d. 2006)
Roberto Suazo Córdova, President of Honduras (1982-86), born in La Paz, Honduras (d. 2018)
Sulkhan Nasidze, Georgian composer, born in Tiflis, Transcaucasia, Soviet Union (now Tblisi, Georgia) (d. 1996)
Edino Krieger, Brazilian conductor, avant-garde composer (Canticum Naturale), and record producer, born in Brusque, Santa Catarina, Brazil (d. 2022)
Eunice Gayson, British actress and 1st Bond Girl (Sylvia Trench- "Dr. No" and "From Russia With Love"), born in London (d. 2018)[1]
William John McKeag, Canadian politician, Lieutenant Governor of Manitoba (1970-76), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 2007)
James Irwin, American air force officer and NASA astronaut (Apollo 15, 8th person to walk on the moon), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1991)
Paul Horn, American jazz and new age flautist, saxophonist, composer and producer (Inside), born in New York City (d. 2014)
David Peakall, British toxicologist whose work on DDT lead to its banning in the US, born in Purley, England (d. 2001)
Lorraine Ellison, American soul singer ("Stay with Me"; "Try (Just a Little Bit Harder)"), and songwriter, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1983)
Dick Curless, American country music singer-songwriter (A Tombstone Every Mile), born in Fort Fairfield, Maine (d. 1995)
Donald N. Langenberg, American physicist, born in Devil's Lake, North Dakota (d. 2019)
Hazel Dukes, American civil rights activist (NAACP president, 1989-92; Spingarn Medal, 2023), born in Montgomery, Alabama (d. 2025)[1]
Myrlie Evers-Williams (née Beasley), American civil rights activist (NAACP chair), born in Vicksburg, Mississippi
Penelope Lively, British writer (Moon Tiger, The Ghost of Thomas Kempe), born in Cairo, Egypt
Erhard Grosskopf, German avant-garde composer, born in Berlin, Germany
Q. T. Macon, American blues singer and guitarist ("Blow Wind"), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1994)
(Patrick Henry) "Adam" Wade, American pop singer (The Writing On The Wall), actor (Kiss Me Goodbye), and 1st US Black game show host (Musical Chairs), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2022)
Vladimir Popović, Serbian soccer midfielder (20 caps, Yugoslavia; Red Star Belgrade) and manager (Intercontinental Cup, Red Star Belgrade 1991), born in Belgrade, Serbia (d. 2020)
Ken Mattingly, American USN naval officer, and NASA astronaut (Apollo 16; STS-4, -51-C), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2023)[1]
